question: What type of material was discovered in at least 15 plastic waste bags during the inspection?
reference_answer: Torn CGMP records, including analytical balance printouts and worksheets containing manufacturing and testing data.
supporting_source_spans: 'Concerns with CGMP records' section
'A large amount of torn CGMP records were discovered in at least 15 plastic waste bags during the inspection, including analytical balance printouts and worksheets...'

question: What did FDA investigators observe in the AHU area, specifically on the air purification units, ducts, a tank, a cleaning unit, and on the floors surrounding multiple units?
reference_answer: Bird droppings and feathers.
supporting_source_spans: Violation 2 (21 CFR 211.58): 'Bird droppings and feathers were observed during the inspection in the AHU area...'

question: What FDA guidance document does the letter point the firm to for data integrity practices?
reference_answer: FDA's guidance document titled 'Data Integrity and Compliance With Drug CGMP'.
supporting_source_spans: 'Concerns with CGMP records' section: 'See FDA's guidance document Data Integrity and Compliance With Drug CGMP...'

question: Into what regulatory category does FDA classify the firm's drug products under section 501(a)(2)(B) of the FD&C Act?
reference_answer: Adulterated.
supporting_source_spans: Opening paragraphs: '...your drug products are adulterated within the meaning of section 501(a)(2)(B) of the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act (FD&C Act), 21 U.S.C. 351(a)(2)(B).'

question: According to the letter, what did swab samples collected from the ducts after the HEPA filters detect?
reference_answer: Residues from multiple previously manufactured drug products and too numerous to count (TNTC) microbial contamination.
supporting_source_spans: Violation 1 (21 CFR 211.67(b)): 'Swab samples collected from the (b)(4) ducts by your firm during the inspection... detected residues from multiple previously manufactured drug products and too numerous to count (TNTC) microbial contamination.'

question: What is the firm's FEI (Facility Establishment Identifier) number cited in the letter?
supporting_source_spans: Opening paragraph naming the inspected facility
Closing instructions: 'Identify your response with FEI 3004097901...'

question: What is the MARCS-CMS number and Warning Letter number assigned to this letter?
reference_answer: MARCS-CMS 697115 and Warning Letter 320-25-48.
supporting_source_spans: Title: 'Granules India Limited MARCS-CMS 697115 — February 26, 2025'
'Warning Letter 320-25-48'

question: Whom must the firm's electronic response identify with by name and credential (in addition to the FEI number)?
reference_answer: Joseph Lambert, Pharm.D.
supporting_source_spans: Closing reply instructions: 'Identify your response with FEI 3004097901 and ATTN: Joseph Lambert, Pharm.D.'

question: On what date range did FDA inspect the facility?
reference_answer: August 26 to September 6, 2024.
supporting_source_spans: Opening paragraph: '...inspected your drug manufacturing facility... from August 26 to September 6, 2024.'

question: On what date did the firm submit its response to the Form FDA 483?
reference_answer: September 27, 2024.
supporting_source_spans: 'We reviewed your September 27, 2024 response to our Form FDA 483...'

question: What is the date of this warning letter?
reference_answer: February 26, 2025.
supporting_source_spans: Header and signature block date: 'February 26, 2025'

question: What is the "Content current as of" date noted at the end of the letter?
supporting_source_spans: End-of-document footer: 'Content current as of: 03/04/2025'

question: At least how many plastic waste bags of torn CGMP records were discovered?
reference_answer: At least 15 plastic waste bags.
supporting_source_spans: 'Concerns with CGMP records' section: 'A large amount of torn CGMP records were discovered in at least 15 plastic waste bags during the inspection...'

question: How many specific numbered CGMP violations are described in the body of the warning letter (each tied to a 21 CFR citation)?
reference_answer: Three (3) numbered CGMP violations — Violation 1 cites 21 CFR 211.67(b); Violation 2 cites 21 CFR 211.58; Violation 3 cites 21 CFR 211.68(a).
supporting_source_spans: Bolded numbered sections '1.', '2.', and '3.' in the body of the letter, each citing a distinct 21 CFR section

question: For which fiscal years does the GDUFA III Commitment Letter referenced in the footnote apply?
reference_answer: Fiscal years (FYs) 2023-2027.
supporting_source_spans: Footnote 1: '...the Generic Drug User Fee Amendments (GDUFA) reauthorization for fiscal years (FYs) 2023-2027, also known as the GDUFA III Commitment Letter...'

question: Within how many working days after receiving the letter must the firm respond?
reference_answer: 15 working days.
supporting_source_spans: 'After you receive this letter, respond to this office in writing within 15 working days.'

question: Who signed the warning letter, and what role within FDA do they hold?
reference_answer: Francis Godwin, Director, Office of Manufacturing Quality, Office of Compliance, Center for Drug Evaluation and Research (CDER).
supporting_source_spans: Signature block: 'Francis Godwin / Director / Office of Manufacturing Quality / Office of Compliance / Center for Drug Evaluation and Research'

question: To which FDA email address is the firm directed to send its electronic reply?
reference_answer: CDER-OC-OMQ-Communications@fda.hhs.gov (Center for Drug Evaluation and Research, Office of Compliance, Office of Manufacturing Quality Communications).
supporting_source_spans: Closing instructions: 'Send your electronic reply to CDER-OC-OMQ-Communications@fda.hhs.gov.'

question: According to the firm's own response cited in the letter, what did reserve sample results confirm regarding contamination?
reference_answer: The firm's response stated that reserve sample results confirmed cross-contamination (though the firm characterized the resulting risk as low based on retrospective MACO calculations).
supporting_source_spans: Violation 1 firm response paragraph: 'Reserve sample results confirmed cross-contamination; however, you deem the risk to drug product safety low based on your retrospective maximum allowable carry-over (MACO) calculations...'

question: How does FDA's assessment of the firm's MACO-based "low risk" argument differ from the firm's own assessment?
reference_answer: The firm deemed the drug product safety risk low based on retrospective MACO calculations and limited reserve sample testing. FDA judged this inadequate, stating cross-contamination is not uniform and limited reserve testing cannot ensure products are contaminant-free; MACO is helpful in validated cleaning scenarios but cannot apply where cleaning has not been performed or exposure is inconsistent; and the denominators used for batch size in the firm's MACO calculations are inconsistent with the size of its (b)(4) bowls.
supporting_source_spans: Violation 1 firm response paragraph (firm's MACO/low-risk position)
Violation 1 FDA rebuttal paragraph: 'Your response is inadequate. Cross-contamination is not uniform...'

question: For each of the three numbered CGMP violations, what is FDA's overall verdict on the adequacy of the firm's Form 483 response?
reference_answer: FDA judged the firm's response inadequate for all three numbered violations. Each violation section concludes with the statement 'Your response is inadequate.'
supporting_source_spans: End of FDA response paragraph in Violation 1
End of FDA response paragraph in Violation 2
End of FDA response paragraph in Violation 3

question: What deficiency does FDA identify in the firm's bird-exclusion remediation efforts, despite the firm cleaning the area and installing netting?
reference_answer: FDA points out that, while the firm cleaned the area and installed netting to block bird entry, the firm failed to perform a thorough root cause analysis or assess similar vulnerabilities elsewhere, and pictures of the netting appear to still allow smaller animals such as insects access to the AHU area.
supporting_source_spans: Violation 2 FDA rebuttal: 'Your response is inadequate. While you address the entry of birds and cleaned the area, you fail to perform a thorough root cause analysis... Furthermore, pictures of the netting you installed appear to still allow smaller animals, such as insects, access to your AHU area.'

question: Does the warning letter name specific affected drug products by name or list specific lot/batch numbers? Why or why not?
reference_answer: No. The letter does not name specific affected drug products or list specific lot/batch numbers. Product names, components, equipment, and similar specifics throughout the violations are redacted as (b)(4), so the document does not identify them.
supporting_source_spans: Throughout violations 1-3: repeated (b)(4) redactions in place of product, equipment, and component identifiers
No section of the letter lists product names or lot/batch numbers

question: Does the letter state that the violations cited are an exhaustive list of all violations at the facility?
reference_answer: No. The letter explicitly states the violations cited are not intended to be an all-inclusive list of violations at the facility, and that the firm is responsible for investigating and determining causes and preventing recurrence or occurrence of other violations.
supporting_source_spans: 'Conclusion' section: 'The violations cited in this letter are not intended to be an all-inclusive list of violations that exist at your facility...'

question: Considering the three numbered CGMP violations together with the separate "Concerns with CGMP records" section, what broader characterization of the firm's quality system does FDA make?
reference_answer: Taken together, the three numbered CGMP violations (inadequate cleaning/maintenance procedures under 21 CFR 211.67(b); failure to maintain buildings in a good state of repair under 21 CFR 211.58; and failure to routinely calibrate/inspect/check equipment under 21 CFR 211.68(a)) and the separate 'Concerns with CGMP records' finding (at least 15 plastic waste bags of torn analytical balance printouts and worksheets) lead FDA to characterize the firm's quality system as not adequately ensuring the accuracy and integrity of data to support the safety, effectiveness, and quality of the drugs manufactured. Combined with the section 501(a)(2)(B) adulteration finding, this describes systemic deficiencies spanning cleaning, facility condition, equipment program, and data integrity.
supporting_source_spans: Violations 1, 2, 3 sections (CGMP citations and conditions)
'Concerns with CGMP records' section, including the explicit quality-system characterization
Opening adulteration statement under FD&C Act 501(a)(2)(B)
